1:
"And the LORD called unto Moses, and spake unto him out of the tabernacle of the congregation, saying," The witnesses note the LORD calling to Moses and speaking out of the tabernacle now reared. Gill notes the LORD called unto Moses, or met him, speaking to him out of the tabernacle, now that it was set up and the glory of the LORD filled it. The witnesses note God calling Moses and speaking to him out of the tabernacle, to give the law of the offerings; the opening of the book of sacrifices, God speaking from the dwelling he had filled with his glory — that, the tabernacle reared, God now speaks from it: "the LORD called unto Moses, and spake unto him out of the tabernacle of the congregation"; the witnesses note the calling, "the LORD called unto Moses" — a gracious calling, that he might draw near to receive God's word; the witnesses note the place, "out of the tabernacle of the congregation" — that God now spoke, not from Sinai, but from the tabernacle, the place of meeting he had just filled with his glory (Ex. 40:34-35); the witnesses note the connexion, that, the dwelling being reared and the glory come, God begins to give from it the laws of the offerings, by which his people might draw near and have communion with him; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that God, having come to dwell among his people, teaches them the way of acceptable approach, for sinful men cannot come to the holy God but by sacrifice; the witnesses note that the way to God is opened by his own appointment; so that God speaks from the tabernacle to give the law of offerings; saying. The witnesses note And the LORD called unto Moses, and spake unto him out of the tabernacle of the congregation. The witnesses note saying.

The witnesses note the LORD calling to Moses and speaking out of the tabernacle now reared and filled with his glory — beginning to give the laws of the offerings, by which his people might draw near; God, having come to dwell among them, teaching the way of acceptable approach, for sinful men cannot come to the holy God but by sacrifice, the way opened by his own appointment.

So the witnesses make the verse the opening of the book of sacrifices, God speaking from his dwelling. The lesson the expositors draw is that God, having come to dwell among his people, now teaches from the tabernacle the way of acceptable approach by sacrifice. The witnesses note the called unto Moses: "the LORD called unto Moses" — Gill notes a gracious calling; the called unto Moses; the a gracious calling; the draw near; the receive his word; the met him. The expositors observe the out of the tabernacle: "out of the tabernacle" — not from Sinai; the out of the tabernacle; the not from Sinai; the the place of meeting; the the glory; the filled it. The witnesses press the the law of offerings: now gives the law of offerings; the the law of offerings; the now gives; the the offerings; the to draw near; the communion. The expositors draw the lesson on the acceptable approach: the way of acceptable approach; the acceptable approach; the the way; the approach; the by sacrifice; the to God. The expositors note the by his appointment: the way opened by his appointment. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the saying, and the law of offerings. The verse thus records the opening of the book of sacrifices — teaching that God speaks from his dwelling to teach the way of acceptable approach by sacrifice.
2:
"Speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, If any man of you bring an offering unto the LORD, ye shall bring your offering of the cattle, even of the herd, and of the flock." The witnesses note God appointing the offerings of the herd and flock for any man who would bring to the LORD. Gill notes the law of sacrifices was given to the children of Israel, not to the Gentiles, and the offering was to be brought of the cattle, of the herd and of the flock. The witnesses note God charging Israel that, if any man would bring an offering to the LORD, it should be of the cattle, the herd or the flock; the appointing of the kinds of offering and the freeness of the bringing — that the offering is of the clean herd and flock, brought by him who will: "If any man of you bring an offering unto the LORD, ye shall bring your offering of the cattle, even of the herd, and of the flock"; the witnesses note the persons, "the children of Israel" — that the law of sacrifices was given to God's covenant people, "not to the Gentiles"; the witnesses note the freeness, "if any man of you bring an offering" — that the bringing was, in this kind, free and voluntary, of him whose heart moved him; the witnesses note the matter, "of the cattle, even of the herd, and of the flock" — that the offering should be of the clean domestic animals, the ox of the herd or the sheep or goat of the flock, such as men possessed and lived by; the witnesses note the type, that these clean victims shadow Christ, the one true offering, of whom all the sacrifices spoke; so that the offering is of the herd and flock, freely brought; even of the herd, and of the flock. The witnesses note Speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, If any man of you bring an offering unto the LORD. The witnesses note ye shall bring your offering of the cattle, even of the herd, and of the flock.

The witnesses note God appointing that any man who would bring to the LORD should bring of the clean herd or flock — the bringing free and voluntary, of him whose heart moved him; God appointing the clean and useful creatures, given of men's own substance at cost to themselves; these victims shadowing Christ, the one true offering, of whom all the sacrifices spoke.

So the witnesses make the verse the appointing of the kinds and the freeness of the bringing. The lesson the expositors draw is that the offering was of the clean herd and flock, brought freely by him who would, a type of Christ the one true offering. The witnesses note the the children of Israel: "the children of Israel" — Gill notes the covenant people; the not the Gentiles. The expositors observe the if any man bring: "if any man of you bring" — free and voluntary; the his heart moved. The witnesses press the herd and flock: "of the herd, and of the flock" — the clean creatures; the men's substance. The expositors draw the lesson on the at cost: given at cost to themselves; the at cost; the given; the at cost; the their substance; the their own. The expositors note the all the sacrifices spoke: of whom all the sacrifices spoke. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ye shall bring your offering of the cattle even of the herd and of the flock, and the offering. The verse thus records the kinds of offering — teaching that it was of the clean herd and flock, freely brought, a type of Christ the one true offering.
3:
"If his offering be a burnt sacrifice of the herd, let him offer a male without blemish: he shall offer it of his own voluntary will at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation before the LORD." The witnesses note the burnt sacrifice of the herd, a male without blemish, offered willingly at the door. Gill notes the burnt sacrifice was so called because consumed by fire, all of it except the skin, offered at the door of the tabernacle. The witnesses note God appointing the burnt sacrifice of the herd to be a male without blemish, offered of the man's own voluntary will at the door before the LORD; the burnt offering, wholly consumed, unblemished, and willingly brought — that the victim is perfect and the offering free: "If his offering be a burnt sacrifice of the herd, let him offer a male without blemish... of his own voluntary will at the door of the tabernacle"; the witnesses note the kind, "a burnt sacrifice," so called because "consumed by fire," all of it (save the skin) burnt to God, the type of entire devotion; the witnesses note the victim, "a male without blemish" — a perfect, unblemished male, the best and the strongest, a type of Christ, the Lamb "without blemish and without spot" (1 Pet. 1:19); the witnesses note the freeness, "of his own voluntary will" — that it was offered willingly, a type of Christ, who "offered himself" freely (Heb. 9:14), and of the believer's free devotion; the witnesses note the place, "at the door of the tabernacle... before the LORD" — the one appointed place of meeting and acceptance; the witnesses note the type, that the whole burnt offering shadows Christ's entire offering of himself to God, a sweet savour, and the believer's whole consecration; so that the burnt offering is perfect, willing, and at the door; before the LORD. The witnesses note If his offering be a burnt sacrifice of the herd, let him offer a male without blemish. The witnesses note he shall offer it of his own voluntary will at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation before the LORD.

The witnesses note the burnt sacrifice — wholly consumed by fire (entire devotion), a male without blemish (a type of Christ, the Lamb without spot), offered of the man's own voluntary will (a type of Christ who offered himself freely) at the door, the one appointed place of acceptance; the whole burnt offering shadowing Christ's entire offering of himself, a sweet savour, and the believer's whole consecration.

So the witnesses make the verse the burnt offering, perfect, willing, and at the door. The lesson the expositors draw is that the victim was unblemished and willingly offered, a type of Christ who offered himself without spot, and the believer's whole consecration. The witnesses note the burnt sacrifice: "a burnt sacrifice" — Gill notes wholly consumed; the burnt sacrifice; the wholly consumed; the by fire; the all of it; the entire devotion. The expositors observe the male without blemish: "a male without blemish" — perfect; the male without blemish; the perfect; the unblemished; the the best; the the Lamb. The witnesses press the voluntary will: "of his own voluntary will" — freely; the Christ offered himself. The expositors draw the lesson on the at the door: "at the door of the tabernacle" — the appointed place; the at the door; the the appointed place; the of meeting; the of acceptance; the the one. The expositors note the whole consecration: the believer's whole consecration. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he shall offer it of his own voluntary will at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation before the LORD, and the burnt offering. The verse thus records the burnt offering — teaching that the victim was unblemished and willingly offered, a type of Christ who offered himself without spot.
4:
"And he shall put his hand upon the head of the burnt offering; and it shall be accepted for him to make atonement for him." The witnesses note the offerer laying his hand on the head of the burnt offering, that it might be accepted to make atonement. Gill notes he was to put his hand, according to the Targum his right hand, on the head of the burnt offering, transferring as it were his guilt and confessing his sins over it. The witnesses note the offerer putting his hand on the head of the burnt offering, that it should be accepted for him to make atonement; the laying on of hands, the substitution of the victim, the great gospel in the offering — that, by the hand laid on, the sin is transferred and the victim accepted in his stead: "he shall put his hand upon the head of the burnt offering; and it shall be accepted for him to make atonement for him"; the witnesses note the act, "put his hand upon the head of the burnt offering" — that the offerer laid his hand (the right hand) on the victim's head, "transferring as it were his guilt" and confessing his sin over it, the victim standing in his stead; the witnesses note the acceptance, "it shall be accepted for him" — that the offering was accepted in the offerer's room, he accepted in and through the sacrifice; the witnesses note the end, "to make atonement for him" — that even the burnt offering, wholly devoted, made atonement, the blood covering sin; the witnesses note that we are accepted not in ourselves but in our Sacrifice; so that the hand laid on transfers the sin, and the victim is accepted; to make atonement for him. The witnesses note And he shall put his hand upon the head of the burnt offering. The witnesses note and it shall be accepted for him to make atonement for him.

The witnesses note the offerer laying his hand on the head of the burnt offering, transferring his guilt and confessing his sin over it, the victim standing in his stead and accepted for him to make atonement — the great type of the transfer of guilt to the substitute, fulfilled in Christ, on whom the LORD laid the iniquity of us all, and the believer's acceptance in the Beloved, accepted not in ourselves but in our Sacrifice.

So the witnesses make the verse the laying on of hands, the substitution of the victim. The lesson the expositors draw is that the offerer transferred his guilt to the victim, which was accepted in his stead to make atonement, a type of Christ bearing our sin and our acceptance in him. The witnesses note the put his hand: "put his hand upon the head" — Gill notes transferring his guilt; the put his hand; the transferring; the his guilt; the confessing; the his sin. The expositors observe the in his stead: the victim in his stead; the in his stead; the the victim; the his stead; the standing; the for him. The witnesses press the accepted for him: "accepted for him" — in the sacrifice; the his room. The expositors draw the lesson on the transfer of guilt: the transfer of guilt to the substitute; the transfer of guilt; the transfer; the guilt; the the substitute; the laid on him. The expositors note the accepted in the Beloved: accepted in the Beloved, not in ourselves. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and it shall be accepted for him to make atonement for him, and the substitution. The verse thus records the laying on of hands — teaching that the offerer's guilt was transferred to the victim, accepted in his stead, a type of Christ bearing our sin and our acceptance in him.
5:
"And he shall kill the bullock before the LORD: and the priests, Aaron's sons, shall bring the blood, and sprinkle the blood round about upon the altar that is by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation." The witnesses note the bullock killed before the LORD and its blood sprinkled round about the altar. Gill notes the man that brought the burnt offering was to kill the bullock, and the priests, Aaron's sons, were to take the blood and sprinkle it round about upon the altar. The witnesses note the offerer killing the bullock before the LORD, and the priests bringing the blood and sprinkling it round about the altar; the death of the victim and the application of the atoning blood — that the substitute is slain and his blood put upon the altar: "he shall kill the bullock before the LORD: and the priests... shall bring the blood, and sprinkle the blood round about upon the altar"; the witnesses note the slaying, "he shall kill the bullock before the LORD" — that the offerer himself slew the victim, the wages of sin being death, the victim dying in his stead; the witnesses note the priests' office, "the priests, Aaron's sons, shall bring the blood" — that the priests received the blood and brought it to the altar, the work of the priest beginning with the blood; the witnesses note the sprinkling, "sprinkle the blood round about upon the altar" — that the blood, the life, was cast all about the altar, presented to God for atonement; the witnesses note that atonement is made by the blood of the substitute; so that the bullock is slain and his blood sprinkled on the altar; that is by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation. The witnesses note he shall kill the bullock before the LORD: and the priests, Aaron's sons, shall bring the blood. The witnesses note and sprinkle the blood round about upon the altar that is by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation.

The witnesses note the offerer killing the bullock before the LORD, the victim dying in his stead, and the priests sprinkling its blood round the altar, presented to God for atonement — the great type of the death of Christ and the application of his blood, for without shedding of blood is no remission; atonement made by the blood of the substitute.

So the witnesses make the verse the death of the victim and the application of the atoning blood. The lesson the expositors draw is that the victim was slain in the offerer's stead and its blood sprinkled on the altar, a type of the death of Christ and the application of his blood. The witnesses note the kill the bullock: "he shall kill the bullock" — Gill notes the offerer slew it; the kill the bullock; the the offerer slew it; the the wages of sin; the death; the in his stead. The expositors observe the bring the blood: "the priests... shall bring the blood" — the priest's office; the to the altar. The witnesses press the sprinkle round about: "sprinkle the blood round about" — for atonement; the the life. The expositors draw the lesson on the death of Christ: the death of Christ; the death of Christ; the the death; the of Christ; the and his blood; the applied. The expositors note the blood of the substitute: atonement by the blood of the substitute. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and sprinkle the blood round about upon the altar that is by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, and the blood. The verse thus records the death and the blood — teaching that the victim was slain in the offerer's stead and its blood sprinkled, a type of the death of Christ and the application of his blood.
6:
"And he shall flay the burnt offering, and cut it into his pieces." The witnesses note the burnt offering flayed and cut into pieces. Gill notes he was to flay the burnt offering, take off its skin, which was the only part not burnt and was the property of the priest, and to cut it into pieces. The witnesses note the offerer flaying the burnt offering and cutting it into its pieces; the preparing of the victim for the altar, laid open and divided — that the whole is opened and parted for the fire: "he shall flay the burnt offering, and cut it into his pieces"; the witnesses note the flaying, "he shall flay the burnt offering" — that the skin was taken off, "the only part... not burnt," which became "the property of the priest" (7:8); the witnesses note the cutting, "cut it into his pieces" — that the body was divided into its parts, the better to be laid on the altar and consumed; the witnesses note the meaning some draw, that the flaying and cutting open of the victim, all laid bare, may shadow the laying open of Christ in his sufferings, nothing hidden, and the searching of the inward parts; the witnesses note that the whole victim, opened and divided, was made ready to be wholly offered to God; the witnesses note that nothing of the burnt offering was kept back, all (save the skin) given to the fire; the witnesses note that the offering is made ready, every part for the altar; so that the burnt offering is flayed and cut in pieces; and cut it into his pieces. The witnesses note And he shall flay the burnt offering. The witnesses note and cut it into his pieces.

The witnesses note the burnt offering flayed (the skin, the only part not burnt, becoming the priest's) and cut into its pieces, the whole opened and parted for the fire — some seeing the laying open of the victim shadow the laying bare of Christ in his sufferings; nothing of the burnt offering kept back, all given to the fire, the offering made ready, every part for the altar.

So the witnesses make the verse the preparing of the victim for the altar. The lesson the expositors draw is that the offering was flayed and cut open, all laid bare and made ready to be wholly offered, nothing kept back. The witnesses note the flay it: "flay the burnt offering" — Gill notes the skin off; the flay it; the the skin off; the not burnt; the the priest's; the the property. The expositors observe the cut into pieces: "cut it into his pieces" — divided; the cut into pieces; the divided; the the parts; the for the altar; the consumed. The witnesses press the laid open: the victim laid open; the laid open; the laid bare; the all; the nothing hidden; the opened. The expositors draw the lesson on the Christ laid bare: Christ laid bare in his sufferings; the Christ laid bare; the Christ; the laid bare; the his sufferings; the searched. The witnesses observe the nothing kept back: nothing kept back; the nothing kept back; the nothing; the kept back; the all to the fire; the the offering. The expositors note the made ready: the offering made ready; the made ready; the made; the ready; the every part; the for the altar. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And he shall flay the burnt offering, the and cut it into his pieces, and the preparing. The verse thus records the preparing of the victim — teaching that it was flayed and cut open, made ready to be wholly offered, nothing kept back.
7:
"And the sons of Aaron the priest shall put fire upon the altar, and lay the wood in order upon the fire:" The witnesses note the priests putting fire on the altar and laying the wood in order. Gill notes the fire of the altar originally came down from heaven and consumed the sacrifice, a token of God's acceptance, and this fire was kept burning continually. The witnesses note the priests putting fire upon the altar and laying the wood in order upon it; the kindling of the altar-fire and the laying of the wood for the burning — that the fire is set and the wood ordered for the sacrifice: "the sons of Aaron the priest shall put fire upon the altar, and lay the wood in order upon the fire"; the witnesses note the fire, "put fire upon the altar" — that the altar-fire "originally came down from heaven" (9:24) and "consumed the sacrifice," a "token of God's acceptance," and was thereafter "kept burning continually" (6:13), never to go out; the witnesses note the wood, "lay the wood in order upon the fire" — that the wood was laid in order, the fuel for the burning; the witnesses note the meaning some draw, that the fire, of heavenly origin and ever burning, may signify the holiness and justice of God, that consumed the offering, and the fervour of the Spirit; the witnesses note that the sacrifice was consumed by a fire of God's own kindling, the token of his acceptance; the witnesses note the type, that Christ's offering was consumed by the fire of divine justice, yet accepted of God; so that the fire is set and the wood laid for the burning; and lay the wood in order upon the fire. The witnesses note And the sons of Aaron the priest shall put fire upon the altar. The witnesses note and lay the wood in order upon the fire.

The witnesses note the priests setting fire on the altar — the fire that came down from heaven, consumed the sacrifice as a token of God's acceptance, and was kept burning continually — and laying the wood in order; some seeing the fire signify the holiness and justice of God that consumed the offering; Christ's offering consumed by the fire of divine justice, yet accepted of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the kindling of the altar-fire and the laying of the wood. The lesson the expositors draw is that the fire, of heavenly origin and ever burning, consumed the sacrifice as a token of acceptance, some seeing the holiness and justice of God. The witnesses note the put fire: "put fire upon the altar" — Gill notes came down from heaven; the put fire; the came down from heaven; the consumed; the the sacrifice; the acceptance. The expositors observe the kept burning: kept burning continually; the kept burning; the continually; the never out; the the fire; the the altar. The witnesses press the lay the wood: "lay the wood in order" — the fuel; the lay the wood; the the fuel; the the burning; the ordered; the for the sacrifice. The expositors draw the lesson on the holiness and justice: the holiness and justice of God; the holiness and justice; the holiness; the justice; the consumed; the the offering. The expositors note the Christ's offering: Christ's offering consumed yet accepted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and lay the wood in order upon the fire, and the fire. The verse thus records the kindling of the fire — teaching that the sacrifice was consumed by a fire of God's own kindling, the token of his acceptance.
8:
"And the priests, Aaron's sons, shall lay the parts, the head, and the fat, in order upon the wood that is on the fire which is upon the altar:" The witnesses note the priests laying the parts, the head, and the fat, in order upon the wood and the fire. Keil and Delitzsch note the priests, Aaron's sons, shall lay the parts, the head and the fat, in order upon the wood that is on the fire upon the altar. The witnesses note the priests laying the parts of the victim, the head and the fat, in order on the wood that was on the fire; the orderly laying of the divided victim upon the altar-fire — that every part is laid in order for the burning: "the priests, Aaron's sons, shall lay the parts, the head, and the fat, in order upon the wood that is on the fire"; the witnesses note the office, "the priests... shall lay the parts" — that the priests, having received the parts, laid them on the altar; the witnesses note the parts, "the head, and the fat," with the rest of the pieces (v.6), each laid "in order upon the wood"; the witnesses note the orderliness, "in order" — that all was done decently and in order, every part set in its place upon the fire; the witnesses note that what is offered to God is to be offered wholly and in order; the witnesses note the type, that Christ offered himself wholly to God, every part devoted; so that the parts are laid in order on the altar-fire; upon the wood that is on the fire which is upon the altar. The witnesses note And the priests, Aaron's sons, shall lay the parts, the head, and the fat. The witnesses note in order upon the wood that is on the fire which is upon the altar.

The witnesses note the priests laying the parts of the victim, the head and the fat, in order on the altar-fire — every part set in its place, all done decently and in order; some seeing the whole victim laid piece by piece signify entire devotion, nothing kept back, the head and the fat all given to God, what is offered to God offered wholly and in order.

So the witnesses make the verse the orderly laying of the divided victim upon the fire. The lesson the expositors draw is that every part was laid in order on the altar, signifying entire devotion, nothing kept back, what is offered to God offered wholly and in order. The witnesses note the lay the parts: "lay the parts" — Keil notes the priests; the lay the parts; the the priests; the received; the on the altar; the the office. The expositors observe the head and fat: "the head, and the fat" — with the pieces; the head and fat; the with the pieces; the the head; the the fat; the the parts. The witnesses press the in order: "in order upon the wood" — every part in its place; the in order; the every part; the in its place; the decently; the the fire. The expositors draw the lesson on the entire devotion: entire devotion, nothing kept back; the entire devotion; the entire; the devotion; the nothing kept back; the all given. The expositors note the wholly and in order: offered wholly and in order. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the in order upon the wood that is on the fire which is upon the altar, and the parts. The verse thus records the laying of the parts — teaching that every part was laid in order, signifying entire devotion, nothing kept back.
9:
"But his inwards and his legs shall he wash in water: and the priest shall burn all on the altar, to be a burnt sacrifice, an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD." The witnesses note the inwards and legs washed, and all burnt on the altar, a sweet savour to the LORD. Gill notes the inwards and legs were washed in water, done first in a room in the court called the washing room, where they washed the inwards of the holy things. The witnesses note the inwards and legs washed in water, and the priest burning all on the altar, a burnt sacrifice of a sweet savour to the LORD; the washing of the inner parts and the wholly burning of the offering, well-pleasing to God — that the whole, made clean, is burnt to God for a sweet savour: "his inwards and his legs shall he wash in water: and the priest shall burn all on the altar... an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD"; the witnesses note the washing, "his inwards and his legs shall he wash in water" — that the inner parts and legs were washed clean, in the "washing room," that nothing unclean should be offered; the witnesses note the burning, "the priest shall burn all on the altar" — that the whole victim, save the skin, was wholly consumed to God, the mark of the burnt offering; the witnesses note the acceptance, "an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD" — that the burning was a "sweet savour," well-pleasing and accepted of God; the witnesses note the great type before given, that the whole burnt offering, washed and wholly consumed, a sweet savour, shadows Christ's offering of himself, "a sweetsmelling savour" (Eph. 5:2), pure within and wholly devoted; so that the whole, washed, is burnt to God for a sweet savour; of a sweet savour unto the LORD. The witnesses note his inwards and his legs shall he wash in water: and the priest shall burn all on the altar, to be a burnt sacrifice. The witnesses note an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD.

The witnesses note the inwards and legs washed clean and the whole burnt on the altar, a sweet savour to the LORD — the washing of the inwards signifying the purity within that God requires, the whole burning the entire devotion of the offering; the great type of Christ's offering of himself, "a sweetsmelling savour," pure within and wholly devoted, well-pleasing to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the washing and the wholly burning, well-pleasing to God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the inwards were washed and the whole burnt, a sweet savour, signifying purity within and entire devotion, a type of Christ's offering. The witnesses note the wash the inwards: "his inwards... shall he wash" — Gill notes the washing room; the wash the inwards; the the washing room; the the inner parts; the clean; the nothing unclean. The expositors observe the burn all: "burn all on the altar" — wholly consumed; the burn all; the wholly consumed; the the whole; the save the skin; the to God. The witnesses press the sweet savour: "a sweet savour unto the LORD" — well-pleasing; the accepted. The expositors draw the lesson on the purity within: the purity within required; the purity within; the the purity; the within; the required; the the inwards. The expositors note the Christ's offering: a sweetsmelling savour, Christ's offering. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the an offering made by fire of a sweet savour unto the LORD, and the offering. The verse thus records the washing and burning — teaching that the whole, made clean, was burnt to God for a sweet savour, a type of Christ's offering.
10:
"And if his offering be of the flocks, namely, of the sheep, or of the goats, for a burnt sacrifice; he shall bring it a male without blemish." The witnesses note the burnt offering of the flock, a male of the sheep or goats without blemish. Matthew Henry notes here are the laws concerning the burnt-offerings of the flock, for those of the middle rank, who could not well afford to offer a bullock, would bring a sheep or a goat. The witnesses note God appointing the burnt offering of the flock, of the sheep or goats, to be a male without blemish; the lesser offering for the middle sort, the same in kind though smaller — that the man of less means brings a sheep or goat: "if his offering be of the flocks... of the sheep, or of the goats, for a burnt sacrifice; he shall bring it a male without blemish"; the witnesses note the kind, "of the flocks... of the sheep, or of the goats" — a smaller victim than the bullock, "for those of the middle rank, who could not well afford to offer a bullock"; the witnesses note the same requirement, "a male without blemish" — that, though smaller, it must still be a perfect, unblemished male, the same in kind and quality, a type of Christ the Lamb without blemish; the witnesses note the gracious provision the commentators draw, that God made room for the offering of every man according to his ability, the middle sort not shut out, but each accepted in his own measure; the witnesses note that God accepts the offering according to what a man hath; the witnesses note that the same Christ is offered, whether the offerer be rich or of middle estate; so that the flock offering is a perfect male, for the middle sort; he shall bring it a male without blemish. The witnesses note And if his offering be of the flocks, namely, of the sheep, or of the goats, for a burnt sacrifice. The witnesses note he shall bring it a male without blemish.

The witnesses note the burnt offering of the flock, a male of the sheep or goats without blemish, for those of the middle rank who could not afford a bullock — smaller, but still perfect and unblemished, a type of Christ the Lamb without blemish; God making room for the offering of every man according to his ability, each accepted in his own measure, the same Christ offered whether the offerer be rich or of middle estate.

So the witnesses make the verse the lesser offering for the middle sort, the same in kind. The lesson the expositors draw is that God made room for each according to his ability, the middle sort bringing a perfect sheep or goat, the same Christ offered. The witnesses note the of the flocks: "of the sheep, or of the goats" — Henry notes the middle rank; the of the flocks; the the middle rank; the could not afford; the a bullock; the smaller. The expositors observe the male without blemish: "a male without blemish" — still perfect; the male without blemish; the still perfect; the unblemished; the the same; the the Lamb. The witnesses press the according to ability: each according to his ability; the room made. The expositors draw the lesson on the not shut out: the middle sort not shut out; the not shut out; the the middle sort; the not shut; the accepted; the his measure. The expositors note the same Christ: the same Christ offered, rich or middle. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he shall bring it a male without blemish, and the flock offering. The verse thus records the offering of the flock — teaching that the middle sort brought a perfect sheep or goat, God making room for each according to his ability.
11:
"And he shall kill it on the side of the altar northward before the LORD: and the priests, Aaron's sons, shall sprinkle his blood round about upon the altar." The witnesses note the flock-victim killed on the north side of the altar and its blood sprinkled round about. Gill notes this is a circumstance not mentioned in the killing of the bullock; Maimonides says there was a square place from the wall of the altar northward where the offerings were slain. The witnesses note the victim of the flock killed on the north side of the altar before the LORD, and the priests sprinkling its blood round about; the slaying in the appointed place and the application of the blood — that the flock-offering too is slain and its blood put on the altar: "he shall kill it on the side of the altar northward before the LORD: and the priests... shall sprinkle his blood round about upon the altar"; the witnesses note the place, "on the side of the altar northward" — that the flock-victim was slain on the "north" side, in the appointed "square place" for killing the offerings, a circumstance here named; the witnesses note the slaying, "before the LORD" — in God's presence, the victim dying in the offerer's stead; the witnesses note the blood, "the priests... shall sprinkle his blood round about upon the altar" — that, as with the bullock, the blood was cast round the altar for atonement; the witnesses note that the same order was kept for the flock as for the herd, the blood ever the ground of atonement; the witnesses note the type before given, that the slaying and the sprinkled blood shadow the death of Christ and the application of his blood; the witnesses note that there is one way of atonement, by the blood of the substitute; so that the flock-victim is slain and its blood sprinkled; shall sprinkle his blood round about upon the altar. The witnesses note And he shall kill it on the side of the altar northward before the LORD. The witnesses note and the priests, Aaron's sons, shall sprinkle his blood round about upon the altar.

The witnesses note the flock-victim killed on the north side of the altar, in the appointed place, and its blood sprinkled round about for atonement — the same order kept for the flock as for the herd, the blood ever the ground of atonement; the slaying and sprinkled blood shadowing the death of Christ and the application of his blood, one way of atonement by the blood of the substitute.

So the witnesses make the verse the slaying in the appointed place and the application of the blood. The lesson the expositors draw is that the flock-victim too was slain and its blood sprinkled, the same order as for the herd, a type of the death of Christ and his blood. The witnesses note the northward: "on the side of the altar northward" — Gill notes the appointed place; the the square place. The expositors observe the before the LORD: "before the LORD" — in his presence; the the victim dying. The witnesses press the sprinkle the blood: "sprinkle his blood round about" — for atonement; the cast. The expositors draw the lesson on the same order: the same order as the herd; the same order; the the same; the order; the the herd; the kept. The expositors note the one way: one way of atonement, by the blood of the substitute. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and the priests Aaron's sons shall sprinkle his blood round about upon the altar, and the blood. The verse thus records the slaying and the blood — teaching that the flock-victim too was slain and its blood sprinkled, a type of the death of Christ.
12:
"And he shall cut it into his pieces, with his head and his fat: and the priest shall lay them in order on the wood that is on the fire which is upon the altar:" The witnesses note the flock-victim cut into pieces and laid in order on the altar-fire. Adam Clarke notes "cut it into his pieces," in the same manner as the bullock, that the parts might be laid in order upon the altar. The witnesses note the flock-victim cut into its pieces, with the head and fat, and the priest laying them in order on the wood and fire; the dividing and orderly laying of the flock-offering, as with the herd — that the lesser victim too is cut and laid in order: "he shall cut it into his pieces, with his head and his fat: and the priest shall lay them in order on the wood that is on the fire"; the witnesses note the cutting, "cut it into his pieces, with his head and his fat" — that the victim was divided "in the same manner as the bullock," head, fat, and the rest; the witnesses note the laying, "the priest shall lay them in order on the wood" — that the parts were laid in order on the altar-fire, as before; the witnesses note that the same order was kept for the flock as for the herd, every part laid in order for the burning; the witnesses note the meaning before given, that the whole victim, divided and laid in order, signifies the entire devotion of the offering, nothing kept back; the witnesses note that what is offered to God is offered wholly and in order, whether the offering be great or small; the witnesses note the type, that Christ offered himself wholly to God; so that the flock-victim is cut and laid in order; on the wood that is on the fire which is upon the altar. The witnesses note And he shall cut it into his pieces, with his head and his fat. The witnesses note and the priest shall lay them in order on the wood that is on the fire which is upon the altar.

The witnesses note the flock-victim cut into its pieces, with the head and fat, and laid in order on the altar-fire, the same order as for the herd — every part laid in order for the burning; the whole victim, divided and laid in order, signifying the entire devotion of the offering, what is offered to God offered wholly and in order, whether great or small.

So the witnesses make the verse the dividing and orderly laying of the flock-offering. The lesson the expositors draw is that the lesser victim too was cut and laid in order, signifying entire devotion, what is offered to God offered wholly and in order. The witnesses note the cut into pieces: "cut it into his pieces" — Clarke notes as the bullock; the cut into pieces; the as the bullock; the the head; the the fat; the divided. The expositors observe the lay in order: "lay them in order" — on the fire; the as before. The witnesses press the same order: the same order as the herd; the kept. The expositors draw the lesson on the entire devotion: entire devotion, nothing kept back; the entire devotion; the entire; the devotion; the nothing kept back; the the offering. The expositors note the Christ offered himself: Christ offered himself wholly to God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and the priest shall lay them in order on the wood that is on the fire which is upon the altar, and the parts. The verse thus records the dividing and laying — teaching that the flock-victim too was cut and laid in order, signifying entire devotion.
13:
"But he shall wash the inwards and the legs with water: and the priest shall bring it all, and offer it upon the altar: it is a burnt sacrifice, an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD." The witnesses note the inwards and legs of the flock-victim washed, and all offered on the altar, a sweet savour to the LORD. Gill notes the inwards and legs were washed with water, as was done with the bullock, before the whole was offered upon the altar. The witnesses note the inwards and legs of the flock-victim washed, the priest bringing and offering all on the altar, a burnt sacrifice of a sweet savour to the LORD; the washing and wholly offering of the flock-offering, as with the herd, well-pleasing to God — that the lesser offering too, washed, is wholly burnt for a sweet savour: "he shall wash the inwards and the legs with water: and the priest shall bring it all, and offer it upon the altar... of a sweet savour unto the LORD"; the witnesses note the washing, "wash the inwards and the legs with water" — that the inner parts were washed clean, "as was done with the bullock," that nothing unclean be offered; the witnesses note the offering, "the priest shall bring it all, and offer it upon the altar" — that the whole was offered, wholly burnt to God; the witnesses note the acceptance, "it is a burnt sacrifice... of a sweet savour unto the LORD" — that this offering too was a sweet savour, well-pleasing and accepted; the witnesses note that the same sweet savour rose from the lesser offering as from the greater, the same Christ being offered; the witnesses note that God accepts the willing and pure offering, great or small; so that the flock-offering, washed, is wholly offered for a sweet savour; of a sweet savour unto the LORD. The witnesses note he shall wash the inwards and the legs with water: and the priest shall bring it all, and offer it upon the altar. The witnesses note it is a burnt sacrifice, an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD.

The witnesses note the inwards and legs of the flock-victim washed and the whole offered on the altar, a sweet savour to the LORD — the offering of the middle sort, though smaller, as fully accepted, "of a sweet savour," as the costlier; the same sweet savour rising from the lesser offering as from the greater, the same Christ offered, God accepting the willing and pure offering, great or small.

So the witnesses make the verse the washing and wholly offering of the flock-offering, well-pleasing to God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the lesser offering, washed and wholly burnt, was as fully accepted, a sweet savour, as the costlier, God accepting according to what a man hath. The witnesses note the wash the inwards: "wash the inwards and the legs" — Gill notes as the bullock; the nothing unclean. The expositors observe the offer it all: "bring it all, and offer it" — wholly burnt; the to God. The witnesses press the sweet savour: "a sweet savour unto the LORD" — well-pleasing; the this offering too. The expositors draw the lesson on the as fully accepted: the lesser as fully accepted; the as fully accepted; the the lesser; the as fully; the accepted; the the costlier. The expositors note the great or small: God accepts the willing offering, great or small. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the it is a burnt sacrifice an offering made by fire of a sweet savour unto the LORD, and the offering. The verse thus records the washing and offering — teaching that the lesser offering too was wholly offered for a sweet savour, as fully accepted as the costlier.
14:
"And if the burnt sacrifice for his offering to the LORD be of fowls, then he shall bring his offering of turtledoves, or of young pigeons." The witnesses note the burnt sacrifice of fowls, turtledoves or young pigeons, for the poorest. Gill notes the offering of fowls was for the poorer sort, who could not offer a bullock, nor a sheep, nor a lamb. The witnesses note God appointing the burnt sacrifice of fowls, turtledoves or young pigeons, for him who could bring no larger offering; the gracious provision for the poorest, that even the poor might bring an acceptable offering — that God makes room for the poor man's offering: "if the burnt sacrifice... be of fowls, then he shall bring his offering of turtledoves, or of young pigeons"; the witnesses note the kind, "of fowls... turtledoves, or... young pigeons" — the smallest and cheapest of offerings, "for the poorer sort, who could not offer a bullock, nor a sheep, or a lamb"; the witnesses note the contrast Ellicott marks, that "the fowls here are in contrast to the cattle," a lesser offering for those of less means; the witnesses note that God accepts the offering of the poor as truly as that of the rich, according to what a man hath; the witnesses note the type, that the same Christ was offered in the poor man's dove as in the rich man's bullock (and that Christ himself, the poor man's Lord, was redeemed with a pair of doves, Luke 2:24); so that the poorest may bring an acceptable offering; or of young pigeons. The witnesses note And if the burnt sacrifice for his offering to the LORD be of fowls. The witnesses note then he shall bring his offering of turtledoves, or of young pigeons.

The witnesses note the burnt sacrifice of fowls — turtledoves or young pigeons — for the poorest, who could not afford a bullock or a sheep; God's gracious provision that even the poor might bring an acceptable offering, none shut out for poverty; the same Christ offered in the poor man's dove as in the rich man's bullock, God accepting according to what a man hath.

So the witnesses make the verse the gracious provision for the poorest. The lesson the expositors draw is that even the poor might bring a pair of doves and be accepted, none shut out for poverty, the same Christ offered. The witnesses note the of fowls: "of fowls... turtledoves, or... young pigeons" — Gill notes the poorer sort; the of fowls; the the poorer sort; the the cheapest; the the smallest; the no bullock. The expositors observe the in contrast to the cattle: "in contrast to the cattle" — a lesser offering; the in contrast to the cattle; the a lesser offering; the less means; the the fowls; the Ellicott. The witnesses press the none shut out: none shut out for poverty; the none shut out; the none; the shut out; the for poverty; the the poorest. The expositors draw the lesson on the accepts the poor: God accepts the poor; the accepts the poor; the accepts; the the poor; the as the rich; the what a man hath. The expositors note the the poor man's Lord: Christ redeemed with doves. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the then he shall bring his offering of turtledoves or of young pigeons, and the poor's offering. The verse thus records the provision for the poorest — teaching that even the poor might bring a pair of doves and be accepted, the same Christ offered.
15:
"And the priest shall bring it unto the altar, and wring off his head, and burn it on the altar; and the blood thereof shall be wrung out at the side of the altar:" The witnesses note the priest bringing the bird, wringing off its head, burning it, and wringing out its blood at the side of the altar. Keil and Delitzsch note the priest shall bring it unto the altar, and wring off its head, and burn it on the altar, and the blood shall be wrung out at the side of the altar. The witnesses note the priest bringing the bird to the altar, wringing off its head, burning it, and wringing out its blood at the side; the offering of the poor man's bird, slain and its blood applied, as with the greater victims — that the bird too is slain and its blood put to the altar: "the priest shall bring it unto the altar, and wring off his head, and burn it on the altar; and the blood thereof shall be wrung out at the side of the altar"; the witnesses note the bringing, to the southeast horn, near the place of the ashes; the witnesses note the slaying, "wring off his head" — that, the bird being small, the priest killed it by wringing or pinching off the head; the witnesses note the blood, "the blood thereof shall be wrung out at the side of the altar" — that the little blood of the bird was pressed out against the altar's side, as the blood of the greater victims was sprinkled; the witnesses note that the same atoning use was made of the poor man's offering, his bird slain and its blood applied; the witnesses note that the poor man's offering was as truly a sacrifice as the rich man's; so that the bird is slain and its blood put to the altar; the blood thereof shall be wrung out at the side of the altar. The witnesses note the priest shall bring it unto the altar, and wring off his head, and burn it on the altar. The witnesses note and the blood thereof shall be wrung out at the side of the altar.

The witnesses note the priest bringing the poor man's bird, wringing off its head, burning it, and pressing out its little blood against the altar's side — the same atoning use made of the poor man's offering as of the greater; the death and blood of the small offering having the same atoning value as the great, the same Christ shadowed, the poor man's offering as truly a sacrifice as the rich man's.

So the witnesses make the verse the offering of the poor man's bird, slain and its blood applied. The lesson the expositors draw is that the bird too was killed and its blood put to the altar, the same atoning use as with the greater victims, the same Christ shadowed. The witnesses note the bring it to the altar: "bring it unto the altar" — Keil notes near the ashes; the the southeast horn. The witnesses press the blood wrung out: "blood... wrung out at the side" — pressed out; the the little blood. The expositors draw the lesson on the same atoning use: the same atoning use; the same atoning use; the the same; the atoning; the use; the the poor's offering. The expositors note the as truly a sacrifice: as truly a sacrifice as the rich man's. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and the blood thereof shall be wrung out at the side of the altar, and the bird. The verse thus records the offering of the bird — teaching that it too was slain and its blood applied, the same atoning use as with the greater victims.
16:
"And he shall pluck away his crop with his feathers, and cast it beside the altar on the east part, by the place of the ashes:" The witnesses note the crop and feathers plucked away and cast beside the altar by the place of the ashes. Gill notes he was to pluck away its crop with its feathers, or with what was in its crop, as Onkelos renders it, the dung, and cast it beside the altar on the east, by the place of the ashes. The witnesses note the priest plucking away the crop with its feathers and casting it beside the altar by the place of the ashes; the removing of the unclean part of the bird before the offering — that what is uncomely is put away: "he shall pluck away his crop with his feathers, and cast it beside the altar on the east part, by the place of the ashes"; the witnesses note the removing, "pluck away his crop with his feathers" — that the crop, with "what was in its crop" (the refuse or "dung"), and the feathers, were plucked away, the unclean and uncomely parts not fit for the altar; the witnesses note the casting, "cast it beside the altar on the east part, by the place of the ashes" — that these were cast aside, into the place where the ashes were laid; the witnesses note the meaning some draw, that, as the inwards of the greater victims were washed, so here the foul part of the bird was removed, that nothing unclean be offered, the offering made pure; the witnesses note that God will have his offerings clean, the refuse cast away; the witnesses note that even in the poor man's small offering, purity was required; so that the uncomely part is removed and cast aside; by the place of the ashes. The witnesses note And he shall pluck away his crop with his feathers. The witnesses note and cast it beside the altar on the east part, by the place of the ashes.

The witnesses note the crop with its refuse and the feathers plucked away and cast aside by the place of the ashes — the unclean and uncomely part removed before the offering; as the inwards of the greater victims were washed, so the foul part of the bird was put away, that nothing unclean be offered, God having his offerings clean, the refuse cast away.

So the witnesses make the verse the removing of the unclean part of the bird. The lesson the expositors draw is that the crop and feathers were plucked away and cast aside, that nothing unclean be offered, purity required even in the poor man's offering. The witnesses note the pluck away the crop: "pluck away his crop with his feathers" — Gill notes the refuse; the pluck away the crop; the the refuse; the the dung; the the feathers; the the uncomely. The expositors observe the cast it aside: "cast it beside the altar" — by the ashes; the cast it aside; the by the ashes; the the east part; the the place; the cast. The witnesses press the nothing unclean: that nothing unclean be offered; the nothing unclean; the nothing; the unclean; the offered; the the foul part. The expositors draw the lesson on the as the inwards washed: as the inwards washed; the as the inwards washed; the as the inwards; the washed; the the foul part; the removed. The expositors note the purity required: purity required even in the poor's offering. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and cast it beside the altar on the east part by the place of the ashes, and the refuse. The verse thus records the removing of the unclean part — teaching that the crop and feathers were cast aside, that nothing unclean be offered.
17:
"And he shall cleave it with the wings thereof, but shall not divide it asunder: and the priest shall burn it upon the altar, upon the wood that is upon the fire: it is a burnt sacrifice, an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD." The witnesses note the bird cleaved by the wings but not divided asunder, and burnt on the altar, a sweet savour to the LORD. Gill notes he was to cleave it with the wings, one wing on one side and the other on the other, but not divide it asunder, the body though cleaved down the middle not parted, nor the wings separated. The witnesses note the priest cleaving the bird by its wings, yet not dividing it asunder, and burning it on the altar, a sweet savour to the LORD; the offering of the poor man's bird, whole and undivided, wholly burnt and well-pleasing — that even the small offering is whole and a sweet savour: "he shall cleave it with the wings thereof, but shall not divide it asunder: and the priest shall burn it upon the altar... of a sweet savour unto the LORD"; the witnesses note the cleaving, "cleave it with the wings thereof" — that the body was cleaved down the middle, the wings left on either side; the witnesses note the limit, "shall not divide it asunder" — that, unlike the greater victims (cut into pieces), the small bird was not parted asunder, but left whole; the witnesses note the burning, "the priest shall burn it upon the altar," wholly consumed; the witnesses note the acceptance, "a burnt sacrifice... of a sweet savour unto the LORD" — that the poor man's bird, wholly burnt, was as truly "a sweet savour" as the rich man's bullock; the witnesses note that the willing and pure offering, though the least, is a sweet savour to God; so that the poor man's bird is wholly burnt, a sweet savour; of a sweet savour unto the LORD. The witnesses note he shall cleave it with the wings thereof, but shall not divide it asunder: and the priest shall burn it upon the altar. The witnesses note it is a burnt sacrifice, an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD.

The witnesses note the bird cleaved by the wings yet not divided asunder, left whole, and wholly burnt on the altar, a sweet savour to the LORD — the poor man's bird as truly "a sweet savour" as the rich man's bullock; God accepting the poor man's offering as fully and sweetly as the rich man's, all according to what a man hath, the same Christ shadowed in great and small.

So the witnesses make the verse the offering of the poor man's bird, whole and well-pleasing. The lesson the expositors draw is that the small offering, wholly burnt, was as truly a sweet savour as the great, God accepting the poor as fully as the rich, the same Christ shadowed. The witnesses note the cleave with the wings: "cleave it with the wings" — Gill notes down the middle; the the wings on each side. The expositors observe the not divide asunder: "shall not divide it asunder" — left whole; the unlike the greater. The expositors draw the lesson on the sweet savour: "a sweet savour unto the LORD" — well-pleasing; the the poor's bird. The expositors note the same Christ: the same Christ shadowed in great and small. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the it is a burnt sacrifice an offering made by fire of a sweet savour unto the LORD, and the bird. The verse thus records the poor man's offering — teaching that the small bird, wholly burnt, was as truly a sweet savour as the great, God accepting the poor as fully as the rich.
